title: Sustainable Value & ESG Readiness

introduction.subtitle: Sustainable value maturity assessment. 7 min.
introduction.description:
Sustainable Value & ESG Readiness helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Materiality & Strategy, Governance & Data, Operations & Value Chain, Claims & Reporting. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

The score stays separate from the optional answer-support check, which asks whether your answers are based on opinion, anecdotes, documentation, or system-verified evidence. A high answer should mean the practice is visible, repeatable, and useful in real decisions.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.duration: 8 diagnostic questions plus 4 evidence checks. Approximately 7 min.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How are material sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities identified?

options.text: A. The area is mostly unmapped, reactive, or dependent on informal trust.
B. The area is known in broad terms, but controls, owners, or evidence are incomplete.
C. Owners, controls, thresholds, and response or remediation paths are documented.
D. Controls are tested, monitored, reviewed after incidents or changes, and integrated into operating governance.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Materiality & Strategy: How are material sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities identified
conceptHelp.summary: Materiality & Strategy means identifying sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities that are material enough to affect strategy or capital allocation. This question narrows the concept to "How are material sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities identified?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Materiality & Strategy matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How are material sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities identified?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How tightly are material sustainability issues connected to strategy and capital allocation?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Materiality & Strategy: How tightly are material sustainability issues connected to strategy and capital allocation
conceptHelp.summary: Materiality & Strategy means identifying sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities that are material enough to affect strategy or capital allocation. This question narrows the concept to "How tightly are material sustainability issues connected to strategy and capital allocation?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How tightly are material sustainability issues connected to strategy and capital allocation?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How clear are governance and accountability?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Governance & Data: How clear are governance and accountability
conceptHelp.summary: Governance & Data means owning sustainability decisions and using reliable baselines, controls, and records instead of loose claims. This question narrows the concept to "How clear are governance and accountability?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Governance & Data matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How clear are governance and accountability?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How reliable are sustainability data and baselines?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Governance & Data: How reliable are sustainability data and baselines
conceptHelp.summary: Governance & Data means owning sustainability decisions and using reliable baselines, controls, and records instead of loose claims. This question narrows the concept to "How reliable are sustainability data and baselines?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Governance & Data matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How reliable are sustainability data and baselines?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Operations & Value Chain; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Operations & Value Chain, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Operations & Value Chain has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Operations & Value Chain is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Operations & Value Chain: How operational are sustainability targets
conceptHelp.summary: Operations & Value Chain means turning sustainability targets into operating changes across product, suppliers, customers, and life cycle. This question narrows the concept to "How operational are sustainability targets?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Operations & Value Chain matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How operational are sustainability targets?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How deeply are suppliers, customers, and product life cycle considered?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Operations & Value Chain: How deeply are suppliers, customers, and product life cycle considered
conceptHelp.summary: Operations & Value Chain means turning sustainability targets into operating changes across product, suppliers, customers, and life cycle. questions.text: How well are external claims substantiated?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Claims & Reporting: How well are external claims substantiated
conceptHelp.summary: Claims & Reporting means making external sustainability claims and reports specific, substantiated, decision-useful, and resistant to greenwashing. This question narrows the concept to "How well are external claims substantiated?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.

D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.

contextQuestions.text: What level of evidence supports your answers for Materiality & Strategy?

options.text: Opinion: mostly judgement, memory, or individual perception.
Anecdotal: examples exist, but they are not systematically documented.
Documented: there are current notes, models, policies, metrics, or records.
System-verified: evidence is routinely produced by a governed workflow, system, or review cadence.

conceptHelp.title: Materiality & Strategy
conceptHelp.summary: This question asks how well your answers about Materiality & Strategy are supported. Materiality & Strategy means identifying sustainability impacts, risks, and opportunities that are material enough to affect strategy or capital allocation. The goal is learning: separate what you know from what you suspect, remember, or still need to verify.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A result about Materiality & Strategy is more useful when you know whether it rests on opinion, a few examples, current documents, or system-verified evidence. Weak support does not make the answer useless; it tells you what to learn next.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the strongest support you actually have right now. Do not upgrade an answer because the practice feels important, and do not downgrade it if current records or systems really exist.
conceptHelp.example: If your Materiality & Strategy answer is based on a recent metric, policy, deal document, customer record, or operating review, choose documented or system-verified. If it is mostly memory or judgement, choose opinion or anecdotal.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
contextDescription: This step is not scored. It records what your diagnostic answers are based on: opinion, anecdote, documentation, or system-verified evidence. Use it to decide how much to verify the result before acting on it.
